name: Petra Treasury
description: The Petra Treasury, also known as Al-Khazneh, is one of the most elaborate temples in Petra, a city carved into sandstone cliffs in southern Jordan. While often referred to as a treasury, its actual purpose remains a subject of debate among historians and archaeologists. Nabataean mythology suggests it served as a mausoleum and a temple dedicated to a significant Nabataean ruler, possibly King Aretas IV (reigned 9 BC to AD 40), or a deified Nabataean goddess. The intricate facade, featuring Hellenistic architectural styles blended with indigenous Nabataean motifs, symbolizes the power and wealth of the Nabataean kingdom. The urn atop the Treasury is rumored to have contained treasure or sacred relics, although it has been opened and found empty. Local Bedouin legends also surround the Treasury, attributing its construction to sorcerers and believing it to be guarded by supernatural entities. The Treasury embodies the Nabataean mastery of rock-cut architecture and their unique blend of cultural influences, reflecting their sophisticated understanding of trade, art, and religious beliefs. The structure's enduring presence serves as a testament to the Nabataean civilization and their profound connection to the landscape of Petra. The elaborate carvings depict deities, mythical creatures, and scenes of the afterlife, contributing to the Treasury's sacred and symbolic significance within Nabataean culture. It represented not only a final resting place but also a gateway to the divine realm, ensuring the continued prosperity and legacy of the deceased ruler and the Nabataean people.
subtitle: Iconic Mausoleum of the Nabataean Kings

keyMyths.title: The Lost Treasure of Al-Khazneh
keyMyths.description: Legend says the urn atop the Treasury contains a vast treasure hidden by the Nabataeans. Many have tried to break it open, but it remains empty, perhaps protected by ancient magic.

keyMyths.title: Aretas IV's Eternal Rest
keyMyths.description: The Treasury is believed to be the final resting place of King Aretas IV, a powerful Nabataean ruler. His spirit is said to still guard the sacred site, ensuring the prosperity of Petra.

sources.author: Jane Taylor
sources.work: Petra and the Lost Kingdom of the Nabataeans
sources.description: A comprehensive guide to Petra, covering its history, architecture, and significance.
sources.text: A comprehensive guide to Petra, covering its history, architecture, and significance.
